coredata.dc:title: Lignin derived reduced fluorescence carbon dots with theranostic approaches: Nano-drug-carrier and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Rai, Sudheer
Singh, Brijesh Kumar
Bhartiya, Prabha
Singh, Anu
Kumar, Hridyesh
Dutta, P.K.
Mehrotra, G.K.
coredata.dc:description: This is the first study on synthesis of water-soluble reduced fluorescent carbon dots (r-FCDs) by using lignosulfonate lignin as carbon source. The resulted r-FCDs have been employed for curcumin nano-carrier and bioimaging applications. The r-FCDs were obtained within 10min under microwave irradiation after reduction with NaBH4. The prepared carbon dots were characterized by various spectroscopic techniques, TEM, XRD, DLS & fluorescence microscopy. The maximum PL intensity was found at 475nm on excitation at 440nm with quantum yield (QYs) of 47.3%. TEM and DLS results showed that average diameter of r-FCDs was about 4.9nm and were readily dispersed in water with strong photostability. XRD results suggested the amorphous nature of r-FCDs. The weak interaction found between an anticancer compound curcumin and surfaces of r-FCDs endowed them with the capacity to act as superior candidate for its capturing and releasing at physiological pH (7.4). The r-FCDs were efficiently up taken by cancerous cells (A549 and SW480) and illuminating the whole cell with a clear distinction between cytoplasm and nucleus. Good biocompatibility results were found with MTT and hemolysis assay. These attractive results indicate that prepared r-FCDs are promising probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lignin
Carbon dots
Theranostic agent
Drug delivery
Bio-imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: pH and Ionic strength stability of CDs
In vitro hemolysis assay of r-FCDs
Synthesis mechanism of r-FCDs from lignin
pH and ionic strength stability of FCDs
Study of curcumin loading on r-FCDs surfaces and its releasing behavior
Cellular uptake and biocompatibility test (MTT and In vitro hemolysis assay)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Different concentrations of lignin (50, 100 and 200mg/25mL) in aqueous media and its carbonized products (FCDs). After reduction with NaBH4 and its changes the visual fluorescence intensity of FCDs solution in UV lamp chamber at short & long wavelength.
Typical fluorescence microscopy images of the fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs). A (bright field), B (excitation wavelength 488nm), C (excitation wavelength 405nm) and D (excitation wavelength 550nm).
Schematic illustration of reduction mechanism of carbon dots.
Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of carbon dots at different excitation wavelength and UV–vis spectrum (in set) of aqueous FCDs.

(A): Typical TEM images of the products (FCDs) and (B): The corresponding carbon dot size distribution measured by dynamic laser light (DLS) scattering using a particle size analyzer (NanoTech ZS 90, Malvern).
The crystalline phase was fluorescent carbon dots were investigated by XRD.
PL spectra of FCDs solution for up to six months and seen remains clear transparent without any precipitation.
Photoluminescence and absorbance of FCDs, r-FCDs and quinine sulfate (results: FCDs = 37.4% and r-FCDs = 47.3% on excited wavelength at 360nm).
PL intensity with spectra of r-CDs at various pH values 2–14.0).
The effect of ionic strength on the r-CDs PL spectra, in NaCl solutions with different concentrations (0–1M), the plot of PL intensity with NaCl variation (inset).
UV–visible spectra of curcumin, r-FCDs and curcumin-loaded r-FCDs at pH 9.2.
Fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin-loaded r-FCDs at different time intervals on excitation wavelength of 440nm from 0 to 72h in PBS pH 7.4.
Percent (%) release of curcumin from curcumin loaded r-CDs with respect to time in PBS with pH of 5.2, 7.4 and 9.2 with control.

Confocal fluorescence microscopy images of cancerous cells (A549 & SW480) labeled with prepared reduced fluorescent carbon dots, A (bright field), B (an overlay image of A & B) and C (excitation at 488nm).
Cytotoxicity evaluation of r-CDs against A549 cells after 48h incubation with different concentration (100 – 500µg/mL) of r-CDs in cell medium as determined by MTT assays.
Hemolysis assay with different concentrations (100–500µg/mL) of r-CDs, PBS (10mM) and Triton X-100 (1%, v/v) act as negative and positive control, respectively.

Preparation procedure and possible forming mechanism of r-FCDs form lignosulfonate lignin in aqueous acidic media, cleavage of β-O-4 (ether) bond and formation of carbocation and repolymerisation reaction.

head.ce:title: Lignin derived reduced fluorescence carbon dots with theranostic approaches: Nano-drug-carrier and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad, Allahabad 211004, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology Allahabad

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This is the first study on synthesis of water-soluble reduced fluorescent carbon dots (r-FCDs) by using lignosulfonate lignin as carbon source. The resulted r-FCDs have been employed for curcumin nano-carrier and bioimaging applications. The r-FCDs were obtained within 10min under microwave irradiation after reduction with NaBH4. The prepared carbon dots were characterized by various spectroscopic techniques, TEM, XRD, DLS & fluorescence microscopy. The maximum PL intensity was found at 475nm on excitation at 440nm with quantum yield (QYs) of 47.3%. TEM and DLS results showed that average diameter of r-FCDs was about 4.9nm and were readily dispersed in water with strong photostability. XRD results suggested the amorphous nature of r-FCDs. The weak interaction found between an anticancer compound curcumin and surfaces of r-FCDs endowed them with the capacity to act as superior candidate for its capturing and releasing at physiological pH (7.4). The r-FCDs were efficiently up taken by cancerous cells (A549 and SW480) and illuminating the whole cell with a clear distinction between cytoplasm and nucleus. Good biocompatibility results were found with MTT and hemolysis assay. These attractive results indicate that prepared r-FCDs are promising probe for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based nanomaterials such as nanoparticles, fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) and carbon nanotubes perform multiple functions like sensing, optical imaging, drug and biomolecules carrier [1–3]. Carbon nanomaterials have been recently explored for their good biocompatibility, high surface area as well as excellent optical properties and bioavailability [4,5]. For example, small sized carbogenic fluorescent nanoparticles (> 10nm) are known as FCDs, an attractive class of carbon nanomaterials [6–8]. Till date, many a lot of work has been published by researchers using various precursors and different synthesis approaches for the fabrication of FCDs. Recently, a lot of synthesis methods have been developed with various strategies such as electrochemical oxidation [9], chemical oxidation [10], laser ablation [11], Pyrolysis [12], arc discharge [13], hydrothermal carbonization [14] and microwave irradiation [5] for preparation of FCDs. Various precursors such as orange peel, soya milk, citric acid, glycerol, kitchen wastes human urine and amino acids have been used in preparation of FCDs, precursors influences the fluorescence property of carbon dots due to varying amount of carbon content and bonding [15–21]. Among the above methods, the microwave irradiation approach has been developed as economically more popular and rapid route.

Another issue regarding the synthesis of low quantum yields of carbon dots. This discrepancy can be fulfilled by doping agents. Doping agents reported to improve the quantum yields (~ 73%) as well as fluorescence property of carbon dots like nitrogen doping [22–24] or sulfur doping [25,26], which is also extensive interest in synthesis of CDs. Many types of functional groups like hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, amino and epoxy groups are present on the surfaces of FCDs which act as both electron acceptors and electron donors or called as oxidizing or reducing agents [27,28]. During synthesis, CDs emerges high oxidation state and further oxidation (oxidative agent) does not facilitate its luminescence property, however reduction of CDs exhibit enhanced fluorescence [29,30]. FCDs have exceptional remarkable properties such as water solubility, high photostability and easy functionalization which make it a promising substitute for organic fluorescent dye or semiconductor quantum dots and used in various applications such as bio-imaging, sensing and drug delivery areas [6–8].

Nanomaterial-based drug delivery system has been an exciting field of research that has more attention, because its low toxicity, selective targeting and ideal drug efficacy as compared with conventional drug delivery systems [31]. Chemotherapy is one of the common treatments for cancer; however, the classic chemotherapeutic agents lack specificity for cancer cells, resulting in high toxicity in normal tissues or organs which are major unsolved task in medical oncology [32]. To overcome this difficulty, several significant efforts have been developed for anticancer drug delivery systems with reduced toxic side effects. Drug delivery to the cancer site can be enhanced with the use of carbon dots which have been shown a promising tool in the chemotherapy due to their small size, high surface area, easy drug loading and releasing capacity against target cells [33,34]. FCDs may be used as an efficient drug carrier because of their well-defined size, three-dimensional structure and various types of functional groups present on their surfaces, which make them attractive candidate for drug delivery and bioimaging. Earlier, various studies have been exploited as optical imaging and carriers to deliver chemotherapeutic agents for cancer therapy such as lisinopril on milk CDs [35], doxorubicin on ferrocene CDs [36], flutamide on vancomycin CDs [37], boldine on dried shrimps CDs [38], cisplatin (IV) on citric acid CDs [39], doxorubicin on phosphorus and nitrogen dual-doped hollow carbon dots (PNHCDs) [40], doxorubicin loaded on citric acid-alanine CDs [41] and telmisartan loaded on chitosan derived CDs [42]. Therefore, development of inexpensive & novel nanomaterial-based drug delivery systems that have dual function of bioimaging and drug carrier is still challenging.

Many biopolymers such as chitosan, cellulose, hemicelluloses and lignin are most abundantly present on earth as waste residue [43,44]. In above, lignin is a natural and aromatic biopolymer obtained as waste by-product from paper-pulp industry and cellulosic ethanol bio-refinery [43,44]. Hence, using lignin as a precursor for more superior applications has been increased quickly in recent years. Due to high carbon content and hydrophobicity, it is growing interest for the development of carbon based nanomaterials from lignin [45,46]. Some difficulties are associated with lignin utilization such as complex aromatic structure, source of origin or type and strong carbon-carbon linkages present in their structure [44–46]. However, little study has been published in scientific articles in association with the development of carbon based nanomaterials from lignin [47–54].

Recently, Chen et al. [54] were the first one who reports on hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from lignin using H2O2 as an oxidizing agent. They used prolong reaction time and high energy consuming procedure (hydrothermal), beside this the resulted CDs were irregular shaped with poor luminescent and less efficient for bio-imaging application and also the quantum yields of resulted CDs were not reported as well as no mention type of lignin precursors used. Despite above these expanding achievements, curcumin-loaded FCDs that act as nanocarriers and as fluorescent probes for controlled drug release and biological optical imaging have not been achieved yet.

In this study, first time we have reported a green and straight forward procedure (microwave-assisted) for the synthesis of FCDs from sulfur containing lignosulfonate lignin as carbon source in which sulfur act doping agent. Synthesized CDs were further converted into reduced carbon dots (r-FCDs) by simply addition of NaBH4 into the solution. Synthesized CDs can serve as a vehicle for drug loading and release because of different types of functional groups present on surface of lignin CDs. It was confirmed that prepared CDs show high stability and fluorescence property with small particle size distribution. Their quantum yields and fluorescent stability were increased after conversion in to r-FCDs. Cell viability and cellular uptake results suggested that the FCDs possess good biocompatibility for in vitro and in vivo imaging (A459 & SW480 cells) and curcumin nanocarrier. The present work provide a novel approach for synthesis of CDs from lignin, and their potential use for bio-imaging & drug carrier applications.

Lignosulfonate lignin powder was purchased from Himedia, India. Curcumin was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium citrate, ascorbic acid, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), quinine sulfate, sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid and sodium chloride were purchased from Sisco Research Laboratories Pvt. Ltd, India. Organic solvents (acetone, ethanol, methanol, isopropanol, chloroform, DMSO), sulfuric acid, sodium carbonate, sodium hydroxide were purchased from Merck, India. Human lung carcinoma cell line (A549) and colon cancer cell line (SW480) were procured from the National Center for Cell Sciences, Pune, India. Dul-becco's modified eagle medium: nutrient mixture Ham's F-12powder (DMEM. F12, Fetal Bovine Serum, FBS), antibiotic antimycotic solution (penicillin, streptomycin and amphotericin-B), Trypsin–EDTA and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) dye were purchased from Invitrogen Co. (Carlsbad, CA, USA). All the chemicals used are of AR grades. Double distilled and Milli-Q grade water were used throughout the experiment.

Lignin FCDs were synthesized by using microwave irradiation method with some modification in previously reported procedure by Chen et al., 2016 [54]. In brief, different concentration (50, 100 and 200mg) of lignosulfonate lignin was dissolved in 25mL of milli Q water and kept for stirring until the homogeneous dispersion of lignin and then ultrasonicated (Leela Sonic-100, India) at 100W for 15min. The mixture was put in 200mL beaker and heated at 600W in a domestic microwave oven (MW73V Samsung, India) for 10min. During microwave irradiation, solution color changed from light brownish liquid to a dark brownish clustered solid. The sample was cooled down to room temperature (naturally) and solid was dissolved with 25mL milli Q water and again sonicated for 30min. Resulted solution was centrifuged (6000rpm, for 10min) to remove large particles and slight brownish supernatant was collected and solution was filtered with 0.2µm nylon membrane syringe filters (AxivaSichem Biotech, India). Filtered solutions were again sonicated for complete dispersion of particles at 100W for 30min. Finally, the obtained luminescent CDs solutions were subjected to dialysis against deionized water via a dialysis membrane (LA395 Himedia, India) pore size of 2.4nm for 48h. The obtained CDs was lyophilized in powder form and kept in 4⁰C prior to use. Next, reduced CDs was synthesized, 0.1gm of NaBH4 was mixed into solution of CDs (1500µg/mL, 100mL) under gently stirred for overnight at room temperature and scheme of overall preparation of r-FCDs shown in Scheme 1. The excess sodium borohydride was removed by dialysis against water.

All fluorescence measurements were recorded in four-walled, all-transparent quartz cell with path length of 10mm by using a Perkin Elmer LS-45 Fluorescence Spectrometer for solutions having absorbance at the wavelength of excitation (λ ex). The sample was excited from 300nm to 580nm, in 20nm increments. UV/visible absorption spectra (200–700nm) were recorded by a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer using slit width of 2nm. A pair of quartz cuvettes with path length of 1cm was employed for this purpose. The surface morphology of prepared CDs was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by using a Tecnai G2 F20 microscope (FEI, USA) at 200kV. The crystalline phase was investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of FCDs by using a Rigaku Smart lab diffractometer in the range of 10–80° at a scan rate of 4°min−1. Typical fluorescence microscopy images of the fluorescent carbon dots and live cell fluorescence images were recorded by an Inverted microscope (EVOS Microscope, USA) at different excitation wavelength (405 and 488nm). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Thermo Nicolet-380 IR spectrophotometer with the KBr pellet technique ranging from 400 to 4000cm−1. Carbon dots solution was lyophilized to powders of by lyophilization in (S.C. Dey. Co. Kolkata, India). pH of various aqueous solutions were checked on a Eutech instruments digital pH meter Bench 700 Series.

The particle size of FCDs was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement using a Nano Microtrac total solution in particle characterization software. The temperature was stabilized to 30±0.1°C of the set temperature. To reduce the influence of larger aggregates the number-average hydrodynamic particle size is reported. The dispersity index is used to describe the width of the particle size distribution, as calculated from the software using a cumulant analysis of the measured intensity auto correlation function.

The ratio of photons absorbed and photons emitted through a fluorescence compounds are called fluorescence quantum yield (QYs). The Quantum yield measurement was performed according to the described comparative procedure with quinine sulfate used as reference compound [32]. Quinine sulfate was dissolved in waster 0.1M H2SO4 (literature reported QYs = 54% or 0.54) and used as references for the calculation of QYs of FCDs aqueous solutions. The QYs of FCDs was calculated through comparing integrated fluorescence intensity (excited at 360nm) and absorbance (less than 0.1 at the excitation wavelength) of each sample. The quantum yield (QYs) of the unknown samples has been calculated by following comparative equation:
QYs
=
QY
R
(
m
x
/
m
R
) × (
n
2
/
n
R
)
Where QYs refers to quantum yield, QYR is the quantum yield of reference compound, m (mx = unknown samples, mR = standard) is slope of the line obtained from the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance 0.01–0.1), n denotes the refractive index of the medium (water, n =1.3). By placing these values in above equation, after calculation we obtained the fluorescence quantum yield for r-FCDs is (QYs) = 0.473 or 47.3%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and Ionic strength stability of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A volume of 500µL of prepared r-FCDs (1mg/mL) was added in 2500µL of milli Q water with different pH values by HCl (2M) or NaOH (2M) was used to adjustment. CDs were mixed in solution and vertexes for 5min. Then, the corresponding PL spectra of r-FCDs at different pH were recorded upon excitation wavelength at 360nm.

Ionic stability of r-FCDs was measured in aqueous solution of NaCl (2.5mL) in different molarity (0 – 1000mM) with mixed 0.5mL of r-FCDs (1mg/mL). PL spectra of the mixture solution were recorded upon excitation wavelength at 360nm.

The in vitro cytotoxicity assay of r-FCDs against A549 cell line was measured by MTT assay [30]. A549 cell line (Human lung carcinoma, NCCS Pune) was maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The cells 100µL were seeded in 96-well tissue culture plates at the density of 1 × 104 cells/well in DMEM media and incubated in CO2 (5%) incubator with at 37⁰C and grown for 48h. The cells were exposed to different concentrations of r-FCDs (100 – 500µg/mL) for 48h at 37⁰C. Then, the 20µL of MTT (5mg/mL prepared in 10mM PBS buffer) solution was added in each well and plate were incubated further for another 4h. A volume of 200µL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added in each well to conform the solubilization of the formazan crystals made. The absorbance was measured at 570nm with microplate reader (Bio-Rad I Mark Microplate reader, USA). Controls cells were cultivated under the same conditions without the exposure of the r-FCDs, only media containing well has been taken as blank to calculate the percentage of cell viability in the treated samples. The percentage of cell viability was calculated by the formula: absorbance of sample - absorbance of blank / absorbance of control – absorbance of blank multiplied by 100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro hemolysis assay of r-FCDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The hemolytic assay of r-FCDs was done based on the established procedure in the literatures [34,38]. Fresh blood was taken in 5mL BD Vacutainer K2 EDTA coated vials (CMDR, MNNIT) and red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by blood centrifugation at 6000rpm for 10min, then washed with PBS. RBCs were dissolved in 2mL PBS and then added 1mL r-FCDs suspended in PBS at different concentration in range of 100–500µg/mL and samples were mixed and incubated it for 3h at room temperature. Same volume of PBS (10mM) and Triton X-100 (1%, v/v) was mixed in RBCs suspension act as negative and positive control, respectively. After that whole samples were again centrifuged at 6000rpm for 10min to obtain the supernatant. Absorbance of collected supernatant (2mL) was measured at 450, 380 and 415nm. The hemoglobin released content or hemolysis (%) in supernatant can be calculated using the following equation.
Supernatant Hb
=
(
2
A
415
)
−
(
A
380
+
A
450 ) × 76 . 25

A549 and SW480 cell line was maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagles Medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). The cells were then incubated in humidified CO2 incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2. The cells were detached from the flask with Trypsin (1mL) after reaching confluency. This cell suspension was centrifuged at 800–1000rpm for 5min and then re-suspended in the growth medium for further study.

For fluorescence studies A549 and SW480 cells were seeded in a 24-well plate at density of 2×104cells/well was trypsinized. Cell cultures plate was incubated inside humidified 5% CO2 incubator for cell attachment at 37°C for 24h, the cells were treated with the r-FCDs at a final concentration of 100μg/mL in 500µL of media and incubated for 12h. Prior to the imaging experiment, the cells were washed three times with fresh media and phosphate buffer saline (PBS) then live-cell-imaging was done under fluorescence inverted microscope (EVOS-FL Microscope) with laser excitation of 405 and 488nm and fluorescence was collected in blue and green region.

A simple method has been implemented for loading of curcumin molecules on r-FCDs. Lignin r-FCDs (500µg/mL) were added in 20mL 1, 4-dioxane-water solution (ratio of 1:1) containing curcumin (0.1mg/mL) and added few drops of acetic acid until the pH (4.0) was achieved. These solutions were mixed in 50mL beaker and stirred for overnight in dark and cool place to promote curcumin binding to r-FCDs. The prepared solution was put in sonicator bath (100W) for 10min. Repeated sonication removes the dioxne from solution. To obtain a dark brownish pellet after centrifugation 12,000rpm for 10min then pellet was then re-suspended in water with the help of sonication. The unbound curcumin molecules were removed from the mixture by dialysis against MQ water for next three days. For the quantification of drug loading efficiency (DLE) supernatant solution was collected to determine through UV–vis absorbance of curcumin at 428nm and compared with the calibration curve was plotted by taking absorbance of curcumin in different concentration (0.1–1mg/mL) in solutions under the same conditions. The prepared sample was lyophilized and obtained in powder form. The quantification of DLE was calculated as follows:
Drug
loading
efficiency
(
%
)
=
Weight
of
curcumin
in
CDs
−
Amount
of
unbound
curcumin
Amount
of
curcumin
in
CDs
×
100

The normal pH of the blood is 7.4, so that in vitro drug release study was examine on different pH (pH = 5.2, 7.4 & 9.2) responsive releasing behavior. In brief, 2.0mg of the curcumin loaded r-FCDs (r-FCDs-curcumin) was dissolved in 20mL PBS buffer solution at different pH (5.2, 7.4 & 9.2). Then 10mL of r-FCDs-curcumin solutions were loaded into a dialysis bag (MWCO 100–500Da) and immersed into 500mL PBS buffer solutions at 37°C water bath and kept in the dark and stirred slowly. At known time intervals, an aliquot of 1.5mL of PBS buffer solution was taken and replaced by same volume of new PBS medium. The release or disassociate of curcumin at various pH was analyzed by UV–visible (λmax = 428nm) spectrophotometer to determine the concentration of dissociated curcumin and the cumulative release rate. Diffusion of free curcumin (same amount) in above solution through the dialysis membrane was used as control. Cumulative release is expressed as the overall percentage of curcumin released through the dialysis membrane against PBS over entire time by using the following formula:
Cumulative
release
=
Total
absorbance
−
Initial
absorbance
Total
absorbance
×
100
Where, total absorbance means is the absorbance intensity at different period i.e. 2, 4, 6, ….72h and initial absorbance is the absorbance intensity at 0h.

Sulfur containing lignin (lignosulfonate) acting both as C-dot precursor as well as a rich source of sulfur as a co-dopant in order to improve the photoluminescence property was first time used for the preparation of carbon dots by microwave-assisted treatment. Different solution of lignin having concentrations 50, 100 and 200mg in 25mL and further ultrasonicated for 15min, were used for synthesis of FCDs.

It was observed that the color intensity of the carbonized products (FCDs) with 4mg/mL concentration (middle vials) under UV lamp chamber showed that highest brightness and conformed its fluorescence property ( Fig. 1). The optical images of the FCDs solutions revealed that the contrast blue fluorescence produced under UV light excitation of short (254nm) and long wavelength (365nm) whereas optical images was perfect different from day light visualization. After the dialysis and syringe filtration the production yields (%) of the FCDs were calculated (initial mass of lignin-FCDs mass/ initial mass of lignin×100) and results were found 2.39%, 5.02% & 4.18% corresponding to different concentrations of lignin 50, 100 and 200mg/25mL, respectively. Some drops of FCDs solution were put on glass slide and dried for examination of fluorescence optical images under fluorescence microscopy with different excitation wavelengths (488, 405, & 550nm). After excitation FCDs emitted different color luminescence and intensities ( Fig. 2) and result was found a uniform size distribution of FCDs was also exhibited by fluorescence microscopy.

The mechanism behind formation of carbon dots from lignin involves the carbonization of its aromatic constituents. In period of synthesis process of FCDs, lignin was catalyzed by weak acid (H2O) at high temperature (more than 100°C) by microwave radiation. Acid hydrolyzes the β-O-4 ether, C-C and hydrogen linkages in lignin into various small fragments in subsequent decomposition or carbonization of lignin [46]. Lignin has innate fluorescence property and after microwave treatment its aromatization and creation of aromatic clumps take place via various reactions (aldol condensation & cyclo addition). When concentration of aromatic clumps reaches a critical saturation position a rupture nucleation takes place and carbon dots are formed. The carbons in FCDs have apparent sp2 hybridization and possess many functional groups such as amino, epoxy, ether, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and carboxylic acid on their surface [54]. Acid causes oxidation steps for the breaking lignin bonding with starting materials and yielding small nanomaterials exhibiting various oxidized functional groups (OH, COOH, SO3, C=O) on surface. Remarkably, while acid oxidation produced FCDs exhibiting shade or light green luminescence, whereas further reaction to sodium borohydride (NaBH4) changed the FCDs’ contrast and intense green luminescence [Fig. 1], attributed to modulation or reducing the functional groups of the surface residues on the FCDs as depicted in Fig. 3. It was already reported that oxidized carbon dots have low quantum yields whereas reduced carbon dots have high quantum yields [60].

In the Fig. 4 showed about the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (PL) emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of the FCDs. It has seen that the UV–vis spectrum (in set) of aqueous FCDs shows that various strong peaks at 301, 368 & 480nm representing the typical absorption of an aromatic moiety and indicating various new types functional groups present on surface and shifting the absorption spectra from low to high wavelength [40]. For the PL spectra, sample was excited from 300nm to 580nm; in 20nm increments and the range of emission spectra were 400 – 530nm found. Note that the dispersion shows a strong PL emission peak and emission maxima was shifted as the excitation wavelength increased and maximum PL intensity at 475nm was found at excitation wavelength of 440nm, and it is indicating the products are fluorescent. The inset shows photographs of the products under visible (left; daylight) and UV light (excitation on 365nm). Fig. 4 shows that PL emission spectra with various excitation wavelengths from 300 to 580nm. It can be seen that the emission spectrum of the products changes (400nm) to (550nm), with a dependence on the excitation wavelengths from 300 to 580nm. The emission peak position shifts to longer wavelengths and the intensity gradually decreases with increased excitation wavelengths. Compared with previously reported C-dots, the products show similar optical properties [45].

TEM images of FCDs are found mono-dispersed pattern and shown in Fig. 5A. The CDs with round shape are superb dispersion in aqueous solution, with the average diameter of 4.6nm (Fig. 5A). As shown in Fig. 5B the average hydrodynamic diameter of the FCDs is determined as 4.9nm by DLS, which is almost very close similar to the result obtained by TEM analysis. The hydrodynamic diameter FCDs obtained by DLS was larger than those by TEM due to presence of solvatation layer around CDs in aqueous solution.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to investigate the crystallinity of carbon dots. The powder XRD patterns of lignin, starting materials ( Fig. 6) depicts three sharp diffraction peaks centered at 19.7°, 38.6° & 60.2°, which are indicative of disordered carbon atoms and crystalline nature. Whereas, broad diffraction peak was found at centered (25°) due to highly disordered carbon atoms present in carbon dots, conforming the amorphous character with highly disordered carbon atoms of synthesized FCDs.

Moreover, the surface functional groups and composition of FCDs were confirmed by FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. 7, the prepared FCDs show the characteristic absorption band at 3357cm−1 is attributed to the O-H stretching vibrations [41]. The absorption bands at 2939 and 1737 & 1600cm−1 are attributed to the characteristics stretching vibrations of C-H stretching (aldehyde), C-O (ketone) and –COOH (carboxylic acid) groups respectively. In addition, the stretching vibration bands at 1509cm−1 clearly indicate that the presence of aromatic ring stretch. Absorption band at 1139 and 1031cm−1 is attributed to the sulfur containing functional groups (sulfones and sulfonic acids). The FCDs solution was kept in freeze (4°C) for six months and seen remains clear transparent without any precipitation and loss of fluorescence property ( Fig. 8).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis mechanism of r-FCDs from lignin
ce:section.ce:para: The main possible synthesis mechanism of r-FCDs is presented in Scheme 1. Lignin is composed of complex aromatic structure and their monomeric units are interlinked by mixture of carbon-carbon and ether linkages in the lignin matrix such as β-O-4, β-1, β-β and β-5 in which β-O-4 linkage as the most rich inter unit linkage [55]. It has been studied that lignin contains various kinds of functional groups such as aliphatic and phenolic hydroxyls, carboxylic, carbonyl, sulfonate and methoxyl groups [55]. The aromatic phenyl rings and these functional groups, together gives a unique properties in lignin [55,56]. During microwave irradiation high temperature was produced in reaction medium. At high temperature water act as weak acid and lignin auto-hydrolysis occurs under acidic condition, carbonium ions are formed in the lignin molecules which attacks especially in the cleavage of β-aryl-ether bonds (β-O-4) that are responsible for the depolymerisation reactions of lignin as shown in Scheme 1 [55–57]. Carbocations have been produced as intermediates products during lignin depolymerisation reactions; acid attacks the cleavage of β-aryl-ether bonds and various types of lignin fragments [55,56]. During repolymerisation of lignin, generated carbocations are electrophilic in nature further reacted with stable and electron rich carbon atoms of the aromatic phenyl rings present in lignin, then high-molecular weight lignin fragments are formed [58]. In the next step, these lignin fragments act as molecular precursors for carbon dots. Consequently, a lignin carbonized aggregates were formed by carbonization reaction. Finally the electrophilic carbon dots were formed and denoted as oxidized carbon dots. The dots emitted in intense green after reduction with NaBH4 and denoted as reduced fluorescent carbon dots (r-FCDs). Fu et al. first time explored the synthesis of carbon dots from aromatic compounds and proofed existence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in the core of carbon dots [59]. These findings carry the assurance that CDs are a type of organic molecular nanocrystal and consisting of many small sp2 hybridized carbon nanodomains fixed within a matrix network comprising mainly sp3-hybridized carbon atoms. The oxygen, sulfur and nitrogen containing functional groups present at the CDs surfaces which enhance the solubility in aqueous medium [59].

By comparing fluorescence spectra & integrated fluorescence intensities (excited at 340nm) of the reduced carbon dots with the references quinine sulfate and determined quantum yield. The values were plotted ( Fig. 9) and the slopes (m value) of the sample and the references were determined. The data showed good linearity with intercepts of around zero and R2 is close to 1. By placing these values in above equation, after calculation we have obtained the fluorescence quantum yield for r-FCDs is (QYs) = 47.3% and FCDs = 31.27% on excited wavelength at 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH and ionic strength stability of FCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photoluminescent intensity (PL) at various pH of r-FCDs was shown in ( Fig. 10). Strong optical responses have been observed during changes in pH. The r-FCDs had elevated stability against wide range of pH variation 2–12. It is clear that the emission intensity attains the maximum value at pH = 2 but it decreases significantly from pH = 2 to pH = 12 and a more changes was found at higher pH values (12−14) in Fig. 10. The various functional groups are present in lignin derived CDs surfaces such as phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl group become to electronegative (bear negative charge) at higher pH condition, which results the change of the absorption peak [61,62]. The stability towards severe pH (12.0) of the resulted CDs exhibited that it has incredible stability in the wide range of pH.

The effect of various ionic strengths on the PL intensity of resulted r-FCDs was also examined in NaCl solution with different concentrations in range of 0–1.0M. As shown in Fig. 11, there are dramatically changes was found in the PL intensities or peak characteristics, when increases gradually ionic strength of NaCI then PL intensities of r-FCDs was partially decreases. The fluorescence intensities remained constant at the certain ionic strength of NaCI (0.2–0.4M) with as depicted in Fig. 11, this showed that it give high fluorescence intensity and can be use in presence of low salt concentrations. Due to the excellent pH and ionic stability of lignin derived r-FCDs can be further explored in areas of sensing and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of curcumin loading on r-FCDs surfaces and its releasing behavior
ce:section.ce:para:
The curcumin molecules loaded on r-FCDs (0.5mg/mL) were obtained by mixing of curcumin (0.1mg/mL) solution in presence of acetic acid. Loading behavior of curcumin on the surfaces of r-FCDs was assessed via UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in (
Figs. 12 and 13). The r-FCDs show the maximum absorbance at 278nm, while pure curcumin shows the absorption peaks at 338 and 352nm. Two new peaks have appeared at 275 and 340nm with increase intensity in curcumin loaded r-FCDs spectra (Fig. 12). It can be observed that the absorption spectra of curcumin significantly changed by conjugation or entrapment with r-FCDs (loaded r-FCDs) in (Fig. 12), which indicating the formation of curcumin–r-CD conjugates. Shifting of spectra from UV to visible region reveals the enhanced conjugation due to formation of conjugate materials. In addition, a slight red shift of UV–vis absorption peak of conjugate materials that attributing to the lower energy required for the n–π* (sulfur) and π–π* transition due to the formation some weak interaction between curcumin-r-FCDs. The fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin-loaded r-FCDs were also reinforced with this conclusion (as shown in Fig. 13). When the curcumin-loaded r-FCDs were excited at 440nm, there was a severe change in the emission spectra at 475nm corresponding to the unbounded r-FCDs, which indicating that the attachment of curcumin on the surfaces of CDs. Based on these results, the drug loading efficiency (%) was also calculated and which was found to be 67.4%. The mechanism behind the attachment between curcumin and r-FCDs are hydrophobic interaction between them. Lignin and curcumin comprises both hydrophilic and hydrophobic groups, the main functional groups present in lignin are the hydroxyl, methoxyl, carbonyl, sulfonate and carboxylic groups [63]. In lignosulfonate lignin it has a non-polar aromatic ring side chain and polar sulfonate or sulfonic acid group, which make it is lipophilic and hydrophilic in nature [64]. Whereas, curcumin is a hydrophobic polyphenolic bioactive compounds having aromatic ring, hydroxyl and alky side chain [65]. The main mechanism of attachment or conjugation between lignin r-FCDs and curcumin may be hydrophobic (major) and hydrogen bonding interaction in presence of acetic acid, because of low pH favored hydrophobic interaction. Therefore, the curcumin loading capacity of r-FCDs may be ascribed by the formation of these interactions on the surfaces of CDs.

In order to examine the release of curcumin from the r-FCDs, PBS solution taken at different time intervals by using dialysis membrane against PBS solution were measured by the UUV–visible and fluorescence spectra. It was indicated that the pH value of the releasing medium can influence the release of the curcumin from carbon dots ( Fig. 14). The release study was done with control sample containing only the curcumin in the free form. Control sample showed more than 80% released in about 2h (Fig. 14). This could confirm that the dialysis membrane was not showed any obstacle during the release study. Conjugation or entrapment of curcumin with r-FCDs exhibited slower release relative to alone. Generally, it was noted that the cytosol of the cells has a pH value ~ 7.4, when the endocytic vesicles entraps the particles upon internalization, cytoplasm have a lower pH that can reach values of 4.0–6.5. So, the pH of the solution plays important role on releasing of drug from the surface of nanomaterials and sensitive functional groups, which acts as activator for the controlled release of drugs. The curcumin-loaded r-FCDs were dialyzed in PBS at pH 5.2, 7.4 and 9.2 for 72h to release of curcumin molecules. The percentage (%) release of curcumin from the r-FCDs was accomplished with support of a curcumin standard calibration curve was shown in (Fig. 14). At biological pH (7.4), the percentage of curcumin release increases gradually and getting a maximum value of 82.7% after 72h. Whereas, at pH 5.2, there was burst release in initial 2h, but after that a steady and slow release profile was observed with increasing time, reaching cumulative percentage release to a maximum value of 69.1% after 72h. Maximum curcumin release (86.37%) was found on pH 9.2, due to the increases hydrophilicity and solubility of curcumin molecules when increased the pH from 5.2 to 9.2, we assume that the release of curcumin from the r-FCDs is due to decrease in hydrophobic strength between curcumin and lignin CDs at high pH. The fluorescence emission strength of the r-FCDs at 475nm is progressively increasing with increasing time from 0 to 72h and maximum at 72h, that confirming the complete releasing of curcumin from the surfaces of CDs, which lets retaining the optical possessions of CDs. These pH dependent release properties may benefit the absorption and release of curcumin when enter into cells and blood. These outcomes indicate that the synthesized CDs can act as a regular drug carrier for basal chemotherapy with fast-acting dosage in slight basic microenvironment. These results may provide a basis for the simultaneous tracking of the drug carriers on their way into cells and the drug release behavior inside the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and biocompatibility test (MTT and In vitro hemolysis assay)
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the application and feasibility of r-FCDs for bio-imaging area, two types of cancerous cells human lung adenocarcinoma (A549) & colon cancer cell line (SW480) cells were used to evaluate the cellular uptake and cytocompatibility with r-FCDs. The obtained r-FCDs by 10min treatment with microwave radiation were introduced into above cancerous cells and their bio-imaging capabilities were evaluated by using inverted fluorescence microscopy. The results showed that the clear fluorescent light was observed in the cytoplasmic area and nucleus of the cell, representing that the r-FCDs were capable to simply diffuse into the cell ( Fig. 15). The A549 cells showed more uptakes of r-FCDs, lightening the whole cell and differentiate the cytoplasm & nucleus clearly. When excited under wavelengths 488nm (green) fluorescence was create in the whole cell cytoplasm. These results have been conformity with earlier studies on the interaction of cells with nanomaterials [27]. The results illustrate that CDs can be used as fluorescence probe for bio-imaging applications.

To investigate the biocompatibility of r-FCDs, MTT assay was performed in A549 human cancer cell lines in order to validate the potential application of the synthesized material for bio-imaging and bio-medical application ( Fig. 16). The cell viability of the r-FCDs was determined by a calorimetric methylthiazoleterazolium (MTT) dye based assay. As per obtained result in Fig. 16, the cell viability was not very much affected by increasing concentration of r-FCDs. The results indicated that r-FCDs exhibit very low cytotoxicity. The cytotoxicity of the r-FCDs were increased slightly with increasing the concentration due to biological origin lignin derived CDs.

Hemocompatibility test performed by hemolysis of RBCs, in which hemolytic agent's lysed the erythrocytes (RBCs), hemoglobin will be out from the erythrocytes and the red color of supernatant was found. Quantity of hemoglobin present (released from RBCs) in the supernatant can be calculated by the absorbance. As shown in Fig. 17, a small hemolysis of RBCs was caused by the r-FCDs, even at high concentration of 800μg/mL, which indicate that good blood compatibility. The percentage of hemolysis (%) was found to be 3.59% at high concentration of r-FCDs (800µg/mL), which is significantly low and safe hemolytic (%) for bio-based materials according to ISO/TR 7406. The above results indicate that lignin derived CDs have less harmful no and more biocompatible.

In summary, simple green approach and cost effective water soluble r-FCD has been successfully developed from natural aromatic carbon source. The lignosulfonate lignin derived r-FCDs were utilized for nanocarrier and bioimaging applications. The emission spectra or PL intensity was found at 475nm when excited with 440nm. The synthesized CDs act as a novel curcumin carrier and show several advantages, such as small sizes (~4.9nm), good biocompatibility, excitation-dependent fluorescence property, least crystallinity & high photostability. The various functional groups (OH, COOH, SO3, =CO) presents on the surfaces of r-FCDs, favouring non-covalent interaction with anticancer compound curcumin. The r-FCDs act as superior candidates for capturing and releasing the curcumin at physiological pH (pH 7.4). Bio-imaging studies were successfully performed with FCDs which showed FCDs were able to feasibly penetrating the in cancerous cells (A549 and SW480) via simple diffusion through cell membrane. The hemolytic ratio of the prepared r-FCDs was found to be 2.89% (at concentration of 0.5mg/mL). These attractive results indicate the prepared r-FCDs are promising nanocarrier for therapeutics and biocompatible probe for cancer diagnosis, hence, lignin based carbon dots may act as a very good theranostic agent for treatment of dreaded disease cancer treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Director, Motilal Nehru National Institute of Technology, Allahabad, India for providing instrumental facilities at the Center for Interdisciplinary Research (CIR).
